patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start with: “I’ve had really bad diarrhea with stomach cramps, and lately I’ve been confused and not myself.” Volunteer upfront: watery diarrhea 8–12x/day for 2 weeks, crampy pain worse before stooling, occasional blood/mucus, low-grade fevers/chills, poor appetite and weight loss, and that family says you’re “acting different.” If asked, add: nighttime diarrhea, urgency/accidents, thirst, dizziness on standing, dry mouth, peeing less, mouth sores, painful red shin bumps, achy knees/ankles, frequent infections, antibiotics 3 weeks ago, recent travel/street food. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, share fears about cost/time off work, and ask for affordable, practical options.

general_concerns: Thinks the episodes are just 'weird dreams' or sleepwalking and not seizures because he rarely loses full awareness and they only happen at night.
Believes seizures must involve full-body shaking; doubts a diagnosis if events are brief, repetitive, and mostly involve sudden arousals, odd movements, or vocalizations.
Assumes stress, caffeine, or screen time 'causes' the problem and that fixing lifestyle alone will cure it without medication.
Worries that taking anti-seizure medication will change his personality or make him 'addicted' and that he’ll need it forever.
Believes if he doesn’t remember the events, they can’t be medically serious; minimizes injury risk and sleep disruption.
Thinks a normal routine EEG would rule out epilepsy and views further testing (video-EEG/polysomnography) as unnecessary.
Assumes it’s not genetic because no one in his immediate family has a formal epilepsy diagnosis; doesn’t recognize that relatives may have been labeled as having 'night terrors' or 'sleepwalking.'
Believes he can safely drive as long as seizures are only nocturnal and he feels fine during the day.
economic_concerns: Fears disclosure will jeopardize his job (on-call duties, security clearance, or being seen as unreliable), so he avoids giving details and resists documentation.
Worries the cost of prolonged video-EEG/sleep study and follow-up visits will be high and wants the 'cheapest test' or to skip testing.
Concerned that a diagnosis will raise insurance premiums or affect eligibility for life/disability coverage.
Afraid medication side effects (fatigue, slowed thinking) will reduce work performance and threaten income.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, 28M. You are vague and evasive, worried this could affect your job/clearance, insurance, and costs. Start with: your partner reports “weird episodes” where you suddenly sit up, look scared, and make repetitive arm/leg movements <1 minute, most nights, sometimes multiple times; you fall back asleep quickly; you feel tired and have bruises/sore muscles; you remember only a split-second panic.
If asked about timing, say it’s usually soon after falling asleep, not near morning. Mention occasional choking/grunting, eyes open but you don’t respond, cheek bites (not tongue), no bedwetting, stress/sleep loss worsens it, teen episodes, family member with ‘night seizures,’ no smell/taste warning, sometimes racing heart.
Do NOT volunteer: near-miss driving, daytime spacing out, irritability/anxiety, headaches, partner sleeping separately, heavy caffeine, sexual movements, and alcohol to sleep—only admit if directly asked, and act embarrassed/defensive.
Offer misconceptions: sleepwalking/night terrors, sleep apnea/snoring, anxiety/ADHD, even a genetic/hormone issue.
If clinician is empathetic, soften slightly, ask for the cheapest test, and worry about meds causing fatigue/slowed thinking.

EEG (overnight video-EEG).value: Interictal: rare right frontal sharp waves during N2 sleep; Ictal: brief (20-40 s) hypermotor events arising from NREM sleep with frontal rhythmic activity; background normal

Brain MRI (epilepsy protocol).value: No structural lesion; normal hippocampi; no cortical dysplasia identified
Brain MRI (epilepsy protocol).normal_range: No acute or epileptogenic structural abnormality

Genetic testing (CHRNA4).value: Heterozygous pathogenic variant detected (consistent with autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy)

medications: carbamazepine ER 200 mg PO at bedtime
comorbidities: Acitretin/etretinate embryopathy (history of in-utero retinoid exposure with craniofacial and skeletal anomalies)
confounders: Parasomnias (sleep terrors) vs nocturnal seizures due to stereotyped hypermotor events during NREM sleep
Obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, nonrestorative sleep) increasing nocturnal arousals and seizure frequency
Alcohol use on weekends and sleep deprivation as seizure triggers
Carbamazepine–acitretin interaction risk: additive hepatotoxicity and potential lipid worsening requiring monitoring
Family history of nocturnal events (parent with similar episodes) may bias toward genetic diagnosis despite overlapping parasomnia features

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact concept of 'autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy' and explicitly links it to 'seizures' that occur predominantly during sleep.

Explains treatment and includes 'carbamazepine' as a key medication option and provides 'seizure precautions' (e.g., driving restrictions per local law, sleep safety, bathing/swimming safety, heights/machinery).
Before prescribing, performs and documents critical safety checks: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; failure to do any is a critical error.
Screens for pregnancy status/contraception potential when relevant and counsels on teratogenic risk and contraception interactions with enzyme-inducing antiseizure medications.

Provides dosing/monitoring plan appropriate for carbamazepine (titration approach, baseline/periodic labs such as CBC and LFTs, and counseling on serious rash/hematologic toxicity warning signs and when to seek urgent care).
Elicits and uses key seizure history (semiology, nocturnal pattern, frequency, duration, triggers, injuries, postictal symptoms) and family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance.
Assesses red flags/alternate diagnoses (syncope, parasomnias, panic/night terrors, psychogenic nonepileptic events) and indicates need for EEG/video-EEG and/or sleep study when uncertainty remains.
Advises lifestyle risk reduction (sleep hygiene, avoiding alcohol/recreational drugs, medication adherence) and provides an emergency plan (when to call EMS, prolonged seizure guidance).
Communicates in clear, non-stigmatizing language, checks understanding (teach-back), and offers written resources and follow-up expectations.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose ADNFLE, start carbamazepine with mandatory safety checks, and refer to neurology/epileptology for confirmation and optimization.
must_collect: family history of similar nocturnal seizures/epilepsy
steps: Explain working diagnosis: autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy and that events are seizures often arising from the frontal lobe during sleep.
Perform allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check prior to prescribing.
Prescribe carbamazepine with a titration plan and counseling on adverse effects and monitoring (CBC/LFTs; rash/fever/sore throat warning signs).
Provide seizure precautions and safety counseling (driving, water safety, heights, operating machinery, supervision during bathing).
Refer to neurology/epileptology for diagnostic confirmation (EEG/video-EEG), consideration of genetics/counseling, and long-term management.
